title: FI-KAN: Fractal Interpolation Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) employ B-spline bases on a fixed grid, providing no intrinsic multi-scale decomposition for non-smooth function approximation. We introduce Fractal Interpolation KAN (FI-KAN), which incorporates learnable fractal interpolation function (FIF) bases from iterated function system (IFS) theory into KAN. Two variants are presented: Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley, 1986) replaces B-splines entirely with FIF bases; Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascues, 2005) retains the B-spline path and adds a learnable fractal correction. The IFS contraction parameters give each edge a differentiable fractal dimension that adapts to target regularity during training. On a Holder regularity benchmark ($α\in [0.2, 2.0]$), Hybrid FI-KAN outperforms KAN at every regularity level (1.3x to 33x). On fractal targets, FI-KAN achieves up to 6.3x MSE reduction over KAN, maintaining 4.7x advantage at 5 dB SNR. On non-smooth PDE solutions (scikit-fem), Hybrid FI-KAN achieves up to 79x improvement on rough-coefficient diffusion and 3.5x on L-shaped domain corner singularities. Pure FI-KAN's complementary behavior, dominating on rough targets while underperforming on smooth ones, provides controlled evidence that basis geometry must match target regularity. A fractal dimension regularizer provides interpretable complexity control whose learned values recover the true fractal dimension of each target. These results establish regularity-matched basis design as a principled strategy for neural function approximation.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Neural function approximation architectures embed implicit assumptions about the regularity of their targets through their choice of basis functions.
Multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) with smooth activation functions (ReLU, SiLU, GELU) construct approximants in spaces of piecewise smooth or analytic functions [ 11 , 20 ] .
Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KAN) [ 27 ] , motivated by the Kolmogorov–Arnold representation theorem [ 24 , 3 ] , replace fixed activations with learnable univariate functions parameterized as B-spline expansions [ 13 ] .
B-splines of order k k reproduce polynomials of degree at most k − 1 k-1 and provide near-optimal approximation rates for targets in Sobolev and Besov spaces with integer or high fractional smoothness.

However, many functions of scientific and engineering interest are not smooth.
Turbulence velocity fields, financial time series, fracture surfaces, natural terrain profiles, and biomedical signals with multi-scale oscillations all exhibit non-trivial Hölder regularity, fractal self-similarity, or nowhere-differentiable character.
For such targets the smooth basis functions used by both MLPs and KANs are fundamentally mismatched: approximating a function with box-counting dimension dim B > 1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}>1 using a smooth basis at resolution h h requires O ​ ( h − 1 / α ) O(h^{-1/\alpha}) basis elements (where α \alpha is the Hölder exponent), with no gain from the polynomial reproduction properties that make splines efficient for smooth targets.

This paper introduces Fractal Interpolation KAN (FI-KAN), which augments or replaces the B-spline bases in KAN with fractal interpolation function (FIF) bases derived from iterated function system (IFS) theory [ 5 , 21 ] .
The key innovation is that the vertical contraction parameters { d i } \{d_{i}\} of the IFS are treated as trainable parameters, giving each edge activation a differentiable fractal dimension that adapts to the regularity structure of the target function during training.

sections.heading: 2.1Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks
sections.text:
The Kolmogorov–Arnold representation theorem [ 24 , 3 ] states that every continuous function f : [ 0 , 1 ] n → ℝ f\colon[0,1]^{n}\to\mathbb{R} admits a representation f ​ ( x 1 , … , x n ) = ∑ q = 0 2 ​ n Φ q ​ ( ∑ p = 1 n ψ q , p ​ ( x p ) ) , f(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=\sum_{q=0}^{2n}\Phi_{q}\!\left(\sum_{p=1}^{n}\psi_{q,p}(x_{p})\right), (1) where Φ q : ℝ → ℝ \Phi_{q}\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} and ψ q , p : [ 0 , 1 ] → ℝ \psi_{q,p}\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R} are continuous univariate functions.
KAN [ 27 ] generalizes this by constructing neural networks whose edges carry learnable univariate functions rather than scalar weights.
Specifically, a KAN layer maps ℝ n in → ℝ n out \mathbb{R}^{n_{\mathrm{in}}}\to\mathbb{R}^{n_{\mathrm{out}}} via 𝐲 = ( ϕ 1 , 1 ​ ( ⋅ ) ⋯ ϕ 1 , n in ​ ( ⋅ ) ⋮ ⋱ ⋮ ϕ n out , 1 ​ ( ⋅ ) ⋯ ϕ n out , n in ​ ( ⋅ ) ) ​ 𝐱 , \mathbf{y}=\begin{pmatrix}\phi_{1,1}(\cdot)&\cdots&\phi_{1,n_{\mathrm{in}}}(\cdot)\\
\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
\phi_{n_{\mathrm{out}},1}(\cdot)&\cdots&\phi_{n_{\mathrm{out}},n_{\mathrm{in}}}(\cdot)\end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}, (2) where each ϕ j , i : ℝ → ℝ \phi_{j,i}\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} is parameterized as a B-spline expansion plus a residual base activation.

In the efficient-KAN implementation [ 7 ] , each edge function takes the form ϕ j , i ​ ( x ) = w j , i ( base ) ​ σ ​ ( x ) + w j , i ( scale ) ​ ∑ m = 0 G + k − 1 w j , i , m ( spline ) ​ B m ( k ) ​ ( x ) , \phi_{j,i}(x)=w^{(\mathrm{base})}_{j,i}\,\sigma(x)+w^{(\mathrm{scale})}_{j,i}\sum_{m=0}^{G+k-1}w^{(\mathrm{spline})}_{j,i,m}B_{m}^{(k)}(x), (3) where σ \sigma is SiLU, { B m ( k ) } \{B_{m}^{(k)}\} are B-spline basis functions of order k k on a grid of size G G , and w ( base ) w^{(\mathrm{base})} , w ( scale ) w^{(\mathrm{scale})} , w ( spline ) w^{(\mathrm{spline})} are learnable parameters.

sections.heading: 2.2Fractal Interpolation Functions
sections.text:
Fractal interpolation, introduced by Barnsley [ 5 ] , constructs continuous functions whose graphs can have prescribed fractal dimension.
The construction uses the theory of iterated function systems (IFS) [ 21 , 6 ] .

Definition 2.1 (Fractal Interpolation Function) . Given interpolation data { ( x i , y i ) } i = 0 N \{(x_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=0}^{N} with a = x 0 < x 1 < ⋯ < x N = b a=x_{0}<x_{1}<\cdots<x_{N}=b , consider the IFS { w i } i = 1 N \{w_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N} on [ a , b ] × ℝ [a,b]\times\mathbb{R} defined by w i ​ ( x , y ) = ( a i 0 c i d i ) ​ ( x y ) + ( e i f i ) , i = 1 , … , N , w_{i}(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix}a_{i}&0\\
c_{i}&d_{i}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\
y\end{pmatrix}+\begin{pmatrix}e_{i}\\
f_{i}\end{pmatrix},\quad i=1,\ldots,N, (4) where: • | d i | < 1 \lvert d_{i}\rvert<1 for all i i (vertical contractivity); • a i , e i a_{i},e_{i} are determined by the interpolation constraints: w i ​ ( x 0 , y 0 ) = ( x i ​ - ​ 1 , y i ​ - ​ 1 ) w_{i}(x_{0},y_{0})=(x_{i\text{-}1},y_{i\text{-}1}) , w i ​ ( x N , y N ) = ( x i , y i ) w_{i}(x_{N},y_{N})=(x_{i},y_{i}) ; • d i ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) d_{i}\in(-1,1) are the vertical scaling (contraction) factors, the only free parameters of the IFS; • c i c_{i} and f i f_{i} are determined by the interpolation constraints and the choice of d i d_{i} : c i = y i − y i − 1 − d i ​ ( y N − y 0 ) x N − x 0 , f i = y i − 1 − c i ​ x 0 − d i ​ y 0 . c_{i}=\frac{y_{i}-y_{i-1}-d_{i}(y_{N}-y_{0})}{x_{N}-x_{0}},\qquad f_{i}=y_{i-1}-c_{i}x_{0}-d_{i}y_{0}. (5) The fractal interpolation function (FIF) f ∗ f^{*} is the unique continuous function whose graph G ​ ( f ∗ ) G(f^{*}) is the attractor of this IFS.

The maps L i ​ ( x ) = a i ​ x + e i L_{i}(x)=a_{i}x+e_{i} project [ x 0 , x N ] [x_{0},x_{N}] onto [ x i − 1 , x i ] [x_{i-1},x_{i}] .
The FIF f ∗ f^{*} satisfies the Read–Bajraktarević (RB) functional equation [ 5 ] : f ∗ ​ ( L i ​ ( x ) ) = c i ​ x + d i ​ f ∗ ​ ( x ) + f i , x ∈ [ x 0 , x N ] , i = 1 , … , N . f^{*}(L_{i}(x))=c_{i}\,x+d_{i}\,f^{*}(x)+f_{i},\quad x\in[x_{0},x_{N}],\quad i=1,\ldots,N. (6)

Theorem 2.2 (Barnsley, 1986 [ 5 ] ) . If | d i | < 1 \lvert d_{i}\rvert<1 for all i = 1 , … , N i=1,\ldots,N , then the IFS ( 4 ) has a unique attractor that is the graph of a continuous function f ∗ : [ a , b ] → ℝ f^{*}\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} satisfying f ∗ ​ ( x i ) = y i f^{*}(x_{i})=y_{i} for all i i .

Theorem 2.3 (Fractal Dimension; Barnsley, 1986 [ 5 ] ) . For the FIF f ∗ f^{*} with c i = 0 c_{i}=0 and | d i | < 1 \lvert d_{i}\rvert<1 , the box-counting dimension of the graph satisfies dim B ( Graph ​ ( f ∗ ) ) = { 1 if ​ ∑ i = 1 N | d i | ≤ 1 , 1 + log ​ ∑ i = 1 N | d i | log ⁡ N if ​ ∑ i = 1 N | d i | > 1 . \dim_{\mathrm{B}}\bigl(\mathrm{Graph}(f^{*})\bigr)=\begin{cases}1&\text{if }\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\leq 1,\\[8.0pt]
1+\dfrac{\log\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert}{\log N}&\text{if }\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert>1.\end{cases} (7)

sections.heading: 2.3Alpha-Fractal Interpolation
sections.text:
Navascués [ 31 ] generalized Barnsley’s construction by introducing the α \alpha -fractal operator.
Given a base function b ∈ C ​ ( [ a , b ] ) b\in C([a,b]) that interpolates the data { ( x i , y i ) } i = 0 N \{(x_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=0}^{N} , the α \alpha -fractal function f b α f_{b}^{\alpha} is defined as the FIF satisfying f b α ​ ( L i ​ ( x ) ) = α i ​ f b α ​ ( x ) + b ​ ( L i ​ ( x ) ) − α i ​ b ​ ( x ) , f_{b}^{\alpha}(L_{i}(x))=\alpha_{i}\,f_{b}^{\alpha}(x)+b(L_{i}(x))-\alpha_{i}\,b(x), (9) where α i ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) \alpha_{i}\in(-1,1) are the fractal parameters and b b is the base function (a classical, typically smooth, approximant).

Setting h = f b α − b h=f_{b}^{\alpha}-b , one obtains h ​ ( L i ​ ( x ) ) = α i ​ h ​ ( x ) , h ​ ( x j ) = 0 ​ for all ​ j , h(L_{i}(x))=\alpha_{i}\,h(x),\quad h(x_{j})=0\text{ for all }j, (10) so h h is a self-affine perturbation that vanishes at the interpolation points.
The α \alpha -fractal function thus decomposes as f b α = b ⏟ classical approximant + h ⏟ fractal perturbation . f_{b}^{\alpha}=\underbrace{b}_{\text{classical approximant}}+\underbrace{h}_{\text{fractal perturbation}}. (11)

This framework has three key properties: 1. Recovery: When α i = 0 \alpha_{i}=0 for all i i , f b α = b f_{b}^{\alpha}=b (the base function is recovered exactly). 2. Continuous bridge: The parameters α i \alpha_{i} provide a continuous transition from classical to fractal approximation. 3. Residual structure: The perturbation h h captures precisely what the smooth base function b b misses. If b b is a spline and the target has fractal structure, then h h encodes the non-smooth residual.

This decomposition provides the theoretical foundation for our Hybrid FI-KAN architecture.

sections.heading: 3FI-KAN Architecture
sections.text: We present two variants of Fractal Interpolation KAN, each grounded in one of the mathematical frameworks described in Section 2 .

sections.heading: 3.1FIF Basis Function Computation
sections.text:
We construct FIF basis functions { φ i ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) } i = 0 N \{\varphi_{i}(x;\mathbf{d})\}_{i=0}^{N} on a uniform grid x i = a + i ​ ( b − a ) / N x_{i}=a+i(b-a)/N for i = 0 , … , N i=0,\ldots,N , using the c i = 0 c_{i}=0 specialization of Barnsley’s framework ( Section 2.2 ).
Evaluation uses a truncated iteration of the RB operator to depth K K .

The boundary corrections at indices 0 and N N enforce the endpoint constraints of the RB operator. The running product r k = ∏ m = 0 k − 1 d σ m r_{k}=\prod_{m=0}^{k-1}d_{\sigma_{m}} tracks the cumulative contraction through k k levels of recursion, where σ m \sigma_{m} is the interval index at recursion depth m m .

Proposition 3.1 (Truncation Error) . Let d max = max i ⁡ | d i | < 1 d_{\max}=\max_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert<1 . The truncated evaluation at depth K K satisfies ∥ f ( K ) − f ∗ ∥ ∞ ≤ C ⋅ d max K , \lVert f^{(K)}-f^{*}\rVert_{\infty}\leq C\cdot d_{\max}^{K}, (12) where C C depends on the interpolation ordinates and the grid.

sections.heading: 3.2Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley Framework)
sections.text:
Pure FI-KAN replaces B-spline basis functions entirely with FIF bases, testing the regularity-matching hypothesis in its strongest form.

Definition 3.2 (Pure FI-KAN Edge Function) . Each edge activation in a Pure FI-KAN layer computes ϕ j , i ​ ( x ) = w j , i ( base ) ​ σ ​ ( x ) + w j , i ( scale ) ​ ∑ m = 0 N w j , i , m ( frac ) ​ φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 i ) , \phi_{j,i}(x)=w_{j,i}^{(\mathrm{base})}\,\sigma(x)+w_{j,i}^{(\mathrm{scale})}\sum_{m=0}^{N}w_{j,i,m}^{(\mathrm{frac})}\,\varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}_{i}), (13) where σ \sigma is SiLU, { φ m ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 i ) } m = 0 N \{\varphi_{m}(\cdot;\mathbf{d}_{i})\}_{m=0}^{N} are the FIF basis functions ( Algorithm 1 ), w j , i , m ( frac ) w^{(\mathrm{frac})}_{j,i,m} are learnable interpolation ordinates (analogous to B-spline coefficients), and 𝐝 i ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) N \mathbf{d}_{i}\in(-1,1)^{N} are the learnable contraction parameters for input feature i i .

sections.heading: 3.3Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascués Framework)
sections.text:
Hybrid FI-KAN instantiates Navascués’s α \alpha -fractal decomposition ( Section 2.3 ) within the KAN architecture: the B-spline path serves as the classical approximant b b and a parallel FIF path provides the fractal correction h h .

Definition 3.3 (Hybrid FI-KAN Edge Function) . Each edge activation computes ϕ j , i ​ ( x ) = w j , i ( base ) ​ σ ​ ( x ) ⏟ base + w j , i ( s . sc . ) ​ ∑ m w j , i , m ( spl ) ​ B m ( k ) ​ ( x ) ⏟ spline path ( = b ) + ∑ m = 0 N w j , i , m ( frac ) ​ φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 i ) ⏟ fractal correction ( ≈ h ) . \phi_{j,i}(x)=\underbrace{w_{j,i}^{(\mathrm{base})}\sigma(x)}_{\text{base}}+\underbrace{w_{j,i}^{(\mathrm{s.sc.})}\sum_{m}w_{j,i,m}^{(\mathrm{spl})}B_{m}^{(k)}(x)}_{\text{spline path}~(=b)}+\underbrace{\sum_{m=0}^{N}w_{j,i,m}^{(\mathrm{frac})}\,\varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}_{i})}_{\text{fractal correction}~(\approx h)}. (14)

This is the α \alpha -fractal decomposition ( 11 ) realized as a neural architecture: f b α ​ ( x ) = b ​ ( x ) ⏟ spline path + h ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) ⏟ fractal correction path . f_{b}^{\alpha}(x)\;=\;\underbrace{b(x)}_{\text{spline path}}\;+\;\underbrace{h(x;\mathbf{d})}_{\text{fractal correction path}}. (15)

sections.heading: 3.4Fractal Dimension Regularization
sections.text:
Both variants support a geometry-aware regularizer derived from IFS theory.

Definition 3.4 (Fractal Dimension Regularizer) . For an edge with contraction parameters 𝐝 = ( d 1 , … , d N ) \mathbf{d}=(d_{1},\ldots,d_{N}) , define R edge ​ ( 𝐝 ) = ( dim B ( 𝐝 ) − 1 ) 2 , dim B ( 𝐝 ) = 1 + log + ⁡ ( ∑ i = 1 N | d i | ) log ⁡ N , R_{\mathrm{edge}}(\mathbf{d})=\bigl(\dim_{\mathrm{B}}(\mathbf{d})-1\bigr)^{2},\qquad\dim_{\mathrm{B}}(\mathbf{d})=1+\frac{\log^{+}\!\bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\bigr)}{\log N}, (17) where log + ⁡ ( x ) = max ⁡ ( log ⁡ x , 0 ) \log^{+}(x)=\max(\log x,0) .
The total fractal regularization loss is ℛ fractal ​ ( θ ) = ∑ ℓ ∑ i R edge ​ ( 𝐝 i ( ℓ ) ) , \mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{fractal}}(\theta)=\sum_{\ell}\sum_{i}R_{\mathrm{edge}}(\mathbf{d}_{i}^{(\ell)}), (18) summed over all layers ℓ \ell and input features i i .

sections.heading: 4Theoretical Analysis
sections.text:
This section develops the approximation-theoretic foundations of FI-KAN.
We establish structural properties of the FIF basis system ( Section 4.1 ), prove convergence of the truncated evaluation ( Section 4.2 ), characterize the variation structure that governs smooth-target performance ( Section 4.3 ), provide approximation rates for Hölder-continuous targets ( Section 4.4 ), analyze the Hybrid architecture as a smooth-rough decomposition ( Section 4.5 ), and establish properties of the fractal dimension regularizer ( Section 4.6 ).

Throughout, we work on a uniform grid x i = a + i ​ h x_{i}=a+ih with h = ( b − a ) / N h=(b-a)/N and use the c i = 0 c_{i}=0 specialization of Barnsley’s IFS.

sections.heading: 4.1Structure of the FIF Basis System
sections.text:
We first establish that the FIF basis functions form a well-defined system with properties analogous to classical interpolation bases.

Theorem 4.1 (FIF Basis Decomposition) . Let 𝐝 = ( d 1 , … , d N ) ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) N \mathbf{d}=(d_{1},\ldots,d_{N})\in(-1,1)^{N} .
There exist unique continuous functions φ 0 , φ 1 , … , φ N : [ a , b ] → ℝ \varphi_{0},\varphi_{1},\ldots,\varphi_{N}\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} such that: (i) Kronecker property: φ j ​ ( x i ; 𝐝 ) = δ i ​ j \varphi_{j}(x_{i};\mathbf{d})=\delta_{ij} for all i , j ∈ { 0 , … , N } i,j\in\{0,\ldots,N\} . (ii) Representation: For any interpolation data { ( x i , y i ) } i = 0 N \{(x_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=0}^{N} , the FIF f ∗ f^{*} with vertical scaling 𝐝 \mathbf{d} and c i = 0 c_{i}=0 satisfies f ∗ ​ ( x ) = ∑ j = 0 N y j ​ φ j ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) . f^{*}(x)=\sum_{j=0}^{N}y_{j}\,\varphi_{j}(x;\mathbf{d}). (20) (iii) Partition of unity: ∑ j = 0 N φ j ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) = 1 \sum_{j=0}^{N}\varphi_{j}(x;\mathbf{d})=1 for all x ∈ [ a , b ] x\in[a,b] . (iv) Degeneration: When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} , φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝟎 ) \varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{0}) is the standard piecewise linear hat function centered at x j x_{j} .

Remark 4.2 . The partition of unity property (iii) is important for numerical stability: the FIF basis values sum to 1 regardless of the contraction parameters, preventing unbounded growth during the forward pass.
Note, however, that unlike B-spline bases, the FIF basis functions φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d}) are not non-negative in general when 𝐝 ≠ 𝟎 \mathbf{d}\neq\mathbf{0} .

The following lemma establishes continuity of the basis system with respect to the contraction parameters, which is essential for gradient-based optimization.

Lemma 4.3 (Continuity in the Contraction Parameters) . The map 𝐝 ↦ φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ) \mathbf{d}\mapsto\varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d}) is continuous from ( − 1 , 1 ) N (-1,1)^{N} to C ​ ( [ a , b ] ) C([a,b]) equipped with the supremum norm.
More precisely, for 𝐝 , 𝐝 ′ ∈ [ − δ , δ ] N \mathbf{d},\mathbf{d}^{\prime}\in[-\delta,\delta]^{N} with δ < 1 \delta<1 : ∥ φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ) − φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ′ ) ∥ ∞ ≤ C j 1 − δ ​ ∥ 𝐝 − 𝐝 ′ ∥ ∞ , \lVert\varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d})-\varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d}^{\prime})\rVert_{\infty}\leq\frac{C_{j}}{1-\delta}\,\lVert\mathbf{d}-\mathbf{d}^{\prime}\rVert_{\infty}, (21) where C j C_{j} depends only on the grid and the index j j .

sections.heading: 4.2Convergence of the Truncated Evaluation
sections.text:
Algorithm 1 computes the FIF bases via a truncated RB iteration.
We now give a precise convergence theorem.

Theorem 4.4 (Truncation Error Bound) . Let φ j ( K ) ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{j}^{(K)}(x;\mathbf{d}) denote the output of Algorithm 1 at depth K K , and let φ j ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{j}(x;\mathbf{d}) denote the exact FIF basis function.
Define d max = max i ⁡ | d i | d_{\max}=\max_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert and S = ∑ i = 1 N | d i | S=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert .
Then for all x ∈ [ a , b ] x\in[a,b] : | φ j ( K ) ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) − φ j ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) | ≤ d max K 1 − d max . \lvert\varphi_{j}^{(K)}(x;\mathbf{d})-\varphi_{j}(x;\mathbf{d})\rvert\leq\frac{d_{\max}^{K}}{1-d_{\max}}. (25) Consequently, the truncated FIF approximation f ∗ ( K ) ​ ( x ) = ∑ j y j ​ φ j ( K ) ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) f^{*(K)}(x)=\sum_{j}y_{j}\varphi_{j}^{(K)}(x;\mathbf{d}) satisfies ∥ f ∗ ( K ) − f ∗ ∥ ∞ ≤ d max K 1 − d max ​ ∥ 𝐲 ∥ 1 . \lVert f^{*(K)}-f^{*}\rVert_{\infty}\leq\frac{d_{\max}^{K}}{1-d_{\max}}\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert_{1}. (26)

Corollary 4.5 . For a target accuracy ε > 0 \varepsilon>0 in the basis evaluation, it suffices to choose K ≥ log ⁡ ( ε ​ ( 1 − d max ) ) log ⁡ d max . K\geq\frac{\log(\varepsilon(1-d_{\max}))}{\log d_{\max}}. (29) For d max = 0.9 d_{\max}=0.9 and ε = 10 − 6 \varepsilon=10^{-6} , this gives K ≥ 13 K\geq 13 .
For d max = 0.5 d_{\max}=0.5 , K ≥ 6 K\geq 6 suffices.

sections.heading: 4.3Total Variation and the Smooth Approximation Obstruction
sections.text:
The fundamental reason that Pure FI-KAN underperforms on smooth targets is that FIF bases with non-trivial contraction parameters have unbounded total variation.
This section makes this obstruction precise.

Definition 4.6 (Total Variation) . For g : [ a , b ] → ℝ g\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} , the total variation is V ​ ( g ) = sup ∑ k = 1 M | g ​ ( t k ) − g ​ ( t k − 1 ) | , V(g)=\sup\sum_{k=1}^{M}\lvert g(t_{k})-g(t_{k-1})\rvert, where the supremum is over all partitions a = t 0 < t 1 < ⋯ < t M = b a=t_{0}<t_{1}<\cdots<t_{M}=b .

Theorem 4.7 (Variation Dichotomy for FIF Bases) . Let φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d}) be a FIF basis function on N N intervals with non-degenerate data (i.e., j ∉ { 0 , N } j\notin\{0,N\} or the endpoint basis is non-trivial). Then: (i) If ∑ i = 1 N | d i | ≤ 1 \sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\leq 1 , then V ​ ( φ j ) < ∞ V(\varphi_{j})<\infty . In particular, when 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} , V ​ ( φ j ) = 2 V(\varphi_{j})=2 (the hat function variation). (ii) If ∑ i = 1 N | d i | > 1 \sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert d_{i}\rvert>1 , then V ​ ( φ j ) = ∞ V(\varphi_{j})=\infty .

The consequence for approximation is immediate:

Corollary 4.8 (Smooth Approximation Obstruction) . Let f ∈ C 2 ​ ( [ a , b ] ) f\in C^{2}([a,b]) and suppose f f is not piecewise linear.
Then for any ε > 0 \varepsilon>0 , if f ∗ ​ ( x ) = ∑ j y j ​ φ j ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) f^{*}(x)=\sum_{j}y_{j}\varphi_{j}(x;\mathbf{d}) with ∑ i | d i | > 1 \sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert>1 satisfies ∥ f − f ∗ ∥ ∞ < ε \lVert f-f^{*}\rVert_{\infty}<\varepsilon , the coefficient vector 𝐲 \mathbf{y} must satisfy ∥ 𝐲 ∥ ∞ ≥ V ​ ( f ) 2 ​ max j ⁡ V ​ ( φ j | [ support ] ) → 0 is impossible when ​ V ​ ( φ j ) = ∞ . \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert_{\infty}\geq\frac{V(f)}{2\max_{j}V(\varphi_{j}|_{[\text{support}]})}\to 0\quad\text{is impossible when }V(\varphi_{j})=\infty. (30) More precisely: the approximation f ∗ = ∑ j y j ​ φ j f^{*}=\sum_{j}y_{j}\varphi_{j} must achieve ∥ f − f ∗ ∥ ∞ < ε \lVert f-f^{*}\rVert_{\infty}<\varepsilon through cancellation of infinite-variation basis functions, which requires the coefficients 𝐲 \mathbf{y} to be tuned to cancel the fractal oscillations at all scales simultaneously.
This cancellation becomes increasingly fragile as the number of grid points increases (each new grid point introduces new oscillatory components), explaining the negative scaling exponents observed experimentally.

Remark 4.9 . Corollary 4.8 provides the rigorous explanation for two experimental observations:
(a) Pure FI-KAN’s negative scaling exponents on smooth targets (e.g., exp_sin): adding grid points adds oscillatory basis functions that make cancellation harder, not easier.
(b) The regularization sweep recovery: forcing 𝐝 → 𝟎 \mathbf{d}\to\mathbf{0} via ℛ fractal \mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{fractal}} eliminates the infinite-variation obstruction, recovering piecewise linear bases with finite variation and 60 × \times improved MSE. This is not a flaw in the architecture. It is the strongest empirical confirmation of the regularity-matching hypothesis: if the basis geometry were irrelevant, smooth-target performance would be independent of 𝐝 \mathbf{d} .

sections.heading: 4.4Approximation Rates for Hölder-Continuous Targets
sections.text:
We now characterize how the choice of basis (B-spline vs. FIF) affects approximation rates for targets of prescribed Hölder regularity.

Definition 4.10 . The Hölder space C 0 , α ​ ( [ a , b ] ) C^{0,\alpha}([a,b]) for α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ] \alpha\in(0,1] consists of continuous functions f f with finite Hölder seminorm: [ f ] α = sup x ≠ y | f ​ ( x ) − f ​ ( y ) | | x − y | α < ∞ . [f]_{\alpha}=\sup_{x\neq y}\frac{\lvert f(x)-f(y)\rvert}{\lvert x-y\rvert^{\alpha}}<\infty. (31)

Theorem 4.11 (B-Spline Approximation of Hölder Functions) . Let f ∈ C 0 , α ​ ( [ a , b ] ) f\in C^{0,\alpha}([a,b]) with α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ] \alpha\in(0,1] .
The best B-spline approximation of order k ≥ 1 k\geq 1 on N N uniform intervals satisfies inf 𝐰 ∥ f − ∑ m w m ​ B m ( k ) ∥ ∞ ≤ [ f ] α ​ h α = [ f ] α ​ ( b − a N ) α , \inf_{\mathbf{w}}\lVert f-\sum_{m}w_{m}B_{m}^{(k)}\rVert_{\infty}\leq[f]_{\alpha}\,h^{\alpha}=[f]_{\alpha}\left(\frac{b-a}{N}\right)^{\alpha}, (32) and this rate is sharp: the exponent α \alpha cannot be improved regardless of the spline order k k .

Theorem 4.12 (FIF Approximation of Self-Affine Targets) . Let f target ∗ f^{*}_{\mathrm{target}} be a FIF on N 0 N_{0} intervals with contraction parameters 𝐝 0 = ( d 0 , 1 , … , d 0 , N 0 ) \mathbf{d}_{0}=(d_{0,1},\ldots,d_{0,N_{0}}) .
Then f target ∗ f^{*}_{\mathrm{target}} can be represented exactly by a Pure FI-KAN with N = N 0 N=N_{0} grid points and matching contraction parameters 𝐝 = 𝐝 0 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{d}_{0} , using N 0 + 1 N_{0}+1 interpolation ordinates y i = f target ∗ ​ ( x i ) y_{i}=f^{*}_{\mathrm{target}}(x_{i}) .

Remark 4.13 . Theorem 4.12 shows that FIF bases can exactly represent self-affine fractal functions with O ​ ( N ) O(N) parameters: N + 1 N+1 ordinates plus N N contraction factors.
In contrast, a B-spline approximation of the same function to accuracy ε \varepsilon requires O ​ ( ε − 1 / α ) O(\varepsilon^{-1/\alpha}) coefficients (by Theorem 4.11 ), where α \alpha is the Hölder exponent of the target.
For Weierstrass-type functions with α ≈ 0.6 \alpha\approx 0.6 , this means the B-spline representation is approximately ε − 1.67 \varepsilon^{-1.67} times larger, a substantial efficiency gap. Of course, real-world targets are not exactly self-affine, so the practical advantage is smaller.
The experimental scaling laws ( Section 5.5 ) quantify the actual gains on specific fractal targets.

sections.heading: 4.5The Hybrid Architecture: An Approximation-Theoretic Guarantee
sections.text:
The Hybrid variant combines the strengths of both basis types.

Theorem 4.14 (Hybrid FI-KAN Approximation Bound) . Let f ∈ C ​ ( [ a , b ] ) f\in C([a,b]) and consider the Hybrid FI-KAN edge function ( 14 ) with B-spline order k k and grid size N N .
Then: (i) Subsumption: inf θ ∥ f − ϕ θ ∥ ∞ ≤ inf 𝐰 ∥ f − b 𝐰 ∥ ∞ \inf_{\theta}\lVert f-\phi_{\theta}\rVert_{\infty}\leq\inf_{\mathbf{w}}\lVert f-b_{\mathbf{w}}\rVert_{\infty} , where b 𝐰 = ∑ m w m ​ B m ( k ) b_{\mathbf{w}}=\sum_{m}w_{m}B_{m}^{(k)} is the best B-spline approximation. (ii) Smooth-rough decomposition: For any decomposition f = g + r f=g+r with g ∈ C s ​ ( [ a , b ] ) g\in C^{s}([a,b]) and r ∈ C 0 , α ​ ( [ a , b ] ) r\in C^{0,\alpha}([a,b]) : inf θ ∥ f − ϕ θ ∥ ∞ ≤ C 1 ​ [ g ] s ​ N − min ⁡ ( s , k ) + inf 𝐲 , 𝐝 ∥ r − ∑ j y j ​ φ j ​ ( ⋅ ; 𝐝 ) ∥ ∞ , \inf_{\theta}\lVert f-\phi_{\theta}\rVert_{\infty}\leq C_{1}\,[g]_{s}\,N^{-\min(s,k)}+\inf_{\mathbf{y},\mathbf{d}}\lVert r-\sum_{j}y_{j}\varphi_{j}(\cdot;\mathbf{d})\rVert_{\infty}, (33) where the spline path approximates g g at the classical rate and the fractal path handles the residual r r . (iii) Strict improvement: If f f has a non-trivial fractal component (i.e., r ≢ 0 r\not\equiv 0 in any optimal decomposition), then there exist 𝐝 ≠ 𝟎 \mathbf{d}\neq\mathbf{0} such that the Hybrid FI-KAN approximation is strictly better than the best B-spline approximation.

sections.heading: 4.6Analysis of the Fractal Dimension Regularizer
sections.text:
We establish analytical properties of the regularizer ℛ fractal \mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{fractal}} defined in Definition 3.4 .

Proposition 4.15 (Regularizer Properties) . Define Δ ​ ( 𝐝 ) = dim B ( 𝐝 ) − 1 = log + ⁡ ( ∑ i | d i | ) / log ⁡ N \Delta(\mathbf{d})=\dim_{\mathrm{B}}(\mathbf{d})-1=\log^{+}\!\bigl(\sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\bigr)/\log N and R ​ ( 𝐝 ) = Δ ​ ( 𝐝 ) 2 R(\mathbf{d})=\Delta(\mathbf{d})^{2} .
Then: (i) Non-negativity: R ​ ( 𝐝 ) ≥ 0 R(\mathbf{d})\geq 0 with equality if and only if ∑ i | d i | ≤ 1 \sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\leq 1 . (ii) Smoothness: R R is continuously differentiable on ( − 1 , 1 ) N ∖ { 𝐝 : ∑ i | d i | = 1 } (-1,1)^{N}\setminus\{\mathbf{d}:\sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert=1\} and Lipschitz on ( − 1 , 1 ) N (-1,1)^{N} . (iii) Gradient: In the active region ∑ i | d i | > 1 \sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert>1 , for d i ≠ 0 d_{i}\neq 0 : ∂ R ∂ d i = 2 ​ Δ ​ ( 𝐝 ) log ⁡ N ⋅ sign ⁡ ( d i ) ∑ j | d j | . \frac{\partial R}{\partial d_{i}}=\frac{2\,\Delta(\mathbf{d})}{\log N}\cdot\frac{\operatorname{sign}(d_{i})}{\sum_{j}\lvert d_{j}\rvert}. (34) (iv) Minimizers: The set of global minimizers is { 𝐝 : ∑ i | d i | ≤ 1 } \{\mathbf{d}:\sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\leq 1\} , a convex polytope in ℝ N \mathbb{R}^{N} .
In the interior of this region, R R is identically zero and exerts no gradient force. (v) Local convexity: R R is convex on the set { 𝐝 : 1 ≤ ∑ i | d i | ≤ e } \{\mathbf{d}:1\leq\sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert\leq e\} , which includes a neighborhood of the transition surface ∑ i | d i | = 1 \sum_{i}\lvert d_{i}\rvert=1 .
In particular, gradient descent on R R efficiently drives 𝐝 \mathbf{d} toward the minimizing set when starting near the transition.

Remark 4.16 . The gradient structure ( 34 ) reveals that the regularizer applies a “democratic” penalty: each contraction parameter d i d_{i} receives gradient proportional to sign ⁡ ( d i ) / ∑ j | d j | \operatorname{sign}(d_{i})/\sum_{j}\lvert d_{j}\rvert , weighted by the current excess dimension Δ \Delta .
This drives all d i d_{i} toward zero at equal rate rather than penalizing outliers (contrast with L 2 L_{2} regularization, which penalizes large parameters quadratically).
Through the tanh \tanh reparameterization d i = 0.99 ​ tanh ⁡ ( d i ( raw ) ) d_{i}=0.99\tanh(d_{i}^{(\mathrm{raw})}) , this becomes a gradient on d i ( raw ) d_{i}^{(\mathrm{raw})} modulated by sech 2 ⁡ ( d i ( raw ) ) \operatorname{sech}^{2}(d_{i}^{(\mathrm{raw})}) , providing natural annealing as d i d_{i} approaches the boundary ± 0.99 \pm 0.99 .

sections.heading: 5.2Core 1D Regression Benchmark
sections.text: Table 1 presents results on all seven 1D target functions.

sections.heading: 5.3Hölder Regularity Sweep
sections.text: To directly test the regularity-matching hypothesis, we evaluate all architectures on the Hölder family f α ​ ( x ) = | x | α f_{\alpha}(x)=\lvert x\rvert^{\alpha} for α \alpha ranging from 0.2 0.2 (very rough) to 2.0 2.0 (smooth).

sections.heading: 5.4Parameter-Matched Comparison
sections.text:
In the default configuration, Hybrid FI-KAN (840 parameters) has approximately twice as many parameters as KAN (416).
To control for this, we increase KAN’s grid size to match the parameter count (grid = 22 {}=22 , yielding 864 parameters).

This rules out the trivial explanation that FI-KAN is better merely because it has more parameters.
At matched parameter counts, the fractal correction provides genuine architectural advantages, particularly on the polynomial ( 13.2 × 13.2\times ) and sawtooth ( 2.5 × 2.5\times ) targets.

sections.heading: 5.5Scaling Laws
sections.text:
We study how test MSE scales with model size by varying the grid size G ∈ { 3 , 5 , 8 , 12 , 16 , 20 } G\in\{3,5,8,12,16,20\} .
Following Liu et al. [ 27 ] , we fit scaling exponents γ \gamma such that MSE ∝ p − γ \mathrm{MSE}\propto p^{-\gamma} where p p is the parameter count.

sections.heading: 5.6Noise Robustness
sections.text: We evaluate robustness by adding Gaussian noise at various signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) to the training targets while testing on clean data.

sections.heading: 5.7Continual Learning
sections.text: We evaluate catastrophic forgetting [ 23 ] by training sequentially on different functions and measuring retention of earlier tasks.

sections.heading: 5.8Regularization Sweep and Fractal Dimension Discovery
sections.text: We sweep the fractal regularization weight λ frac ∈ { 0 , 10 − 4 , 10 − 3 , 10 − 2 , 10 − 1 , 1 } \lambda_{\mathrm{frac}}\in\{0,10^{-4},10^{-3},10^{-2},10^{-1},1\} on two targets: polynomial (smooth, true dim B = 1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1 ) and Weierstrass (fractal, true dim B ≈ 1.36 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}\approx 1.36 ).

sections.heading: 5.9Fractal Depth Analysis
sections.text: We vary the recursion depth K K in the Hybrid FI-KAN on the Weierstrass target to study the trade-off between basis expressiveness and optimization difficulty.

sections.heading: 5.102D Regression
sections.text:
Both FI-KAN advantages extend to multiple dimensions.
Pure FI-KAN wins on the 2D fractal target (0.125 vs. KAN 0.187) but loses on Ackley (which is smooth outside the origin).
Hybrid FI-KAN wins both, with a 14.6 × 14.6\times improvement on the Ackley function.

sections.heading: 5.11Non-Smooth PDE Solutions
sections.text:
The preceding experiments use synthetic target functions with prescribed regularity.
We now test FI-KAN on solutions of partial differential equations whose non-smooth character arises from the PDE structure itself: corner singularities, rough coefficients, and noise-driven roughness.
All reference solutions are computed by established numerical methods: scikit-fem [ 18 ] for finite element solutions and the fbm package [ 16 ] for fractional Brownian motion coefficient fields.
The regression task is to learn the mapping from spatial coordinates to the solution value, bypassing physics-informed loss functions entirely.

sections.heading: 5.12Fractal Dimension as Diagnostic Tool
sections.text: The learned fractal dimensions from both variants correlate with the regularity of the target, providing an interpretable diagnostic.

sections.heading: 5.13Training Dynamics of Fractal Parameters
sections.text:
To understand how FI-KAN adapts its basis geometry during training, we track the evolution of the contraction parameters d i d_{i} and the resulting fractal dimension dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} across epochs ( Fig. 17 ).
The training dynamics reveal three distinct regimes.
On smooth targets (left panel), the Pure variant’s d i d_{i} values grow unchecked (without sufficient regularization), pushing dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} above 1.2 and wasting representational capacity on spurious multi-scale structure; this is precisely the pathology predicted by Corollary 4.8 .
On fractal targets with regularization (center panel), the parameters find a stable equilibrium near dim B = 1.0 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1.0 , balancing expressiveness against complexity.
The sawtooth case (right panel) is particularly instructive: the early-training spike in dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} to ∼ 1.05 \sim\!1.05 followed by consolidation suggests an explore-then-consolidate dynamic where the network first probes fractal parameter space and then retains only what reduces the loss.

sections.heading: 8Conclusion
sections.text:
We have introduced Fractal Interpolation KAN (FI-KAN), which incorporates learnable fractal interpolation bases from iterated function system theory into the Kolmogorov–Arnold Network framework.
Two variants, Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley framework) and Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascués framework), provide complementary perspectives on the regularity-matching hypothesis: the geometric regularity of the basis functions should be adapted to the geometric regularity of the target function.

The experimental evidence supports this hypothesis across multiple axes.
On fractal and non-smooth targets, FI-KAN provides up to 6.3 × 6.3\times MSE reduction over KAN on genuine fractal targets ( dim B = 1.5 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1.5 ).
On non-smooth PDE solutions with structured roughness, computed via finite elements ( scikit-fem ) and exact spectral methods, Hybrid FI-KAN achieves up to 79 × 79\times improvement over KAN, with the strongest gains on diffusion equations with rough coefficients and corner singularity problems.
The Hölder regularity sweep demonstrates consistent improvements across the full regularity spectrum.
The fractal dimension regularizer provides interpretable, geometry-aware complexity control whose learned values correlate with true target regularity.
An additional advantage in noise robustness ( 6.1 × 6.1\times on fractal targets) suggests that fractal bases provide structural benefits beyond pure approximation quality.

The contrast between Pure and Hybrid FI-KAN is itself a scientific finding: it demonstrates that basis geometry genuinely matters, and that the optimal choice depends on the regularity of the target.
A further distinction emerges between structured and unstructured roughness: FI-KAN’s advantage requires that the target’s non-smooth character exhibit learnable multi-scale correlations (as in PDE-inherited roughness or deterministic fractal functions), rather than purely stochastic roughness from single random path realizations.
This establishes regularity-matched basis design as a viable and principled strategy for neural function approximation, opening the door to architectures that explicitly adapt their geometric structure to the problem at hand.
The immediate practical implications lie in scientific domains where non-smooth structure is the rule rather than the exception: subsurface flow in heterogeneous media, elliptic PDEs on non-convex domains, stochastic PDE solutions, geophysical signal processing, and biomedical signals with fractal scaling.
In each of these settings, the ability to learn the appropriate regularity from data, rather than assuming smoothness a priori, addresses a fundamental modeling gap that current neural architectures leave open.

sections.heading: A.1KAN Paper Toy Functions
sections.text:
On smooth, low-dimensional targets ( J 0 J_{0} Bessel, e x ​ sin ⁡ ( y ) + y 2 e^{x}\sin(y)+y^{2} , x ⋅ y x\cdot y ), KAN’s B-spline bases are near-optimal and the fractal correction provides no advantage.
The one Hybrid FI-KAN win (10D sum) suggests that fractal bases may help in higher dimensions even for relatively smooth targets, possibly by providing additional representational diversity.

Pure FI-KAN performs substantially worse on all five targets ( Table 13 ), consistent with the approximation-theoretic analysis in Section 4 .

sections.heading: A.2Feynman Physics Equations
sections.text:
On smooth physics equations, KAN wins 4 of 7, consistent with the expectation that B-splines are well-adapted to analytic targets.
The FI-KAN wins (Gaussian, relativity, diffraction) occur on functions with sharp transitions or oscillatory structure that benefit from multi-scale representation.

figures.caption: Figure 1 : MLPs vs. KANs vs. FI-KANs. (a) MLP: scalar weights on edges, fixed activations on nodes. (b) KAN: learnable B-spline functions on edges. (c) Pure FI-KAN: replaces B-splines with fractal interpolation bases φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}) . (d) Hybrid FI-KAN: retains B-splines and adds a fractal correction ( f b α = b + h f_{b}^{\alpha}=b+h ). When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} , (d) reduces to (b).

Figure 2 : FI-KAN: learning basis geometry across the smooth-to-rough spectrum. Top row: FI-KAN combines smooth basis functions (B-spline/hat-type),
fractal interpolation with learnable contraction parameters 𝐝 \mathbf{d} ,
and the KAN edge-function graph to produce regularity-matched edge geometry. Middle row: Smooth-to-fractal basis morphing as d i d_{i} increases from 0 to 0.9 0.9 .
At d i = 0 d_{i}=0 the basis is a smooth hat function ( dim B = 1 \dim_{B}=1 ); as d i d_{i} increases,
the basis acquires progressively finer self-affine structure with dim B > 1 \dim_{B}>1 . Bottom row: The two FI-KAN variants.
Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley framework) uses all-fractal bases, strongest on rough targets.
Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascués framework) retains a spline backbone and adds a fractal correction,
providing robustness across both smooth and rough regimes.

Figure 3 : Edge function architecture for the three models. (a) KAN : base activation plus B-spline path. (b) Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley): replaces B-splines with fractal interpolation bases φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}) . When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} , the FIF bases reduce to piecewise linear hat functions. (c) Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascués): retains the B-spline path and adds a fractal correction, implementing the α \alpha -fractal decomposition f b α = b + h f_{b}^{\alpha}=b+h . When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} and fractal weights vanish, Hybrid reduces to standard KAN.

Figure 4 : Fractal basis function φ 2 ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{2}(x;\mathbf{d}) (the middle basis on a 5-interval grid) as the contraction parameters d i d_{i} vary uniformly from 0 to 0.9. At d i = 0 d_{i}=0 , the basis is the standard piecewise linear hat function ( dim B = 1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1 ). As d i d_{i} increases, the basis acquires increasingly fine-scale fractal structure ( dim B > 1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}>1 ) while maintaining the Kronecker property φ 2 ​ ( x j ) = δ 2 ​ j \varphi_{2}(x_{j})=\delta_{2j} at all grid points (black dots). Recursion depth K = 8 K=8 .

Figure 5 : Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley framework) architecture detail. (a) Network graph: all edges carry fractal interpolation function (FIF) bases φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}) with learnable contraction parameters; nodes perform summation. (b) Edge activation: the input x x splits into a base SiLU path and a fractal path ∑ w m frac ​ φ m ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \sum w_{m}^{\mathrm{frac}}\varphi_{m}(x;\mathbf{d}) , where 𝐝 ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) N \mathbf{d}\in(-1,1)^{N} controls the
fractal character of the basis.
When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} , the FIF bases reduce to piecewise linear hat functions (order-1 KAN). (c) Fractal basis φ 2 ​ ( x ; 𝐝 ) \varphi_{2}(x;\mathbf{d}) at four contraction values d i ∈ { 0 , 0.3 , 0.6 , 0.9 } d_{i}\in\{0,0.3,0.6,0.9\} , computed via K = 10 K=10 truncated Read–Bajraktarević iterations.
The Kronecker property φ 2 ​ ( x j ) = δ 2 ​ j \varphi_{2}(x_{j})=\delta_{2j} is preserved at all d d values (dots). (d) Box-counting dimension dim B ( 𝐝 ) \dim_{B}(\mathbf{d}) as a function of uniform d i d_{i} ,
with the transition surface ∑ | d i | = 1 \sum|d_{i}|=1 marked. (e) Fractal dimension regularizer ℛ ​ ( 𝐝 ) = ( dim B ( 𝐝 ) − 1 ) 2 \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{d})=(\dim_{B}(\mathbf{d})-1)^{2} :
a geometry-aware Occam’s razor that penalizes unnecessary fractal complexity.

Figure 6 : Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascués framework) architecture detail. (a) Network graph: edges carry dual paths—solid blue for the B-spline path b b (classical approximant) and dashed coral for the fractal correction h h —implementing
the α \alpha -fractal decomposition f b α = b + h f_{b}^{\alpha}=b+h . (b) Edge activation: the input splits into three paths (base SiLU, B-spline, FIF),
combined into the α \alpha -fractal output f b α = b + h f_{b}^{\alpha}=b+h .
When 𝐝 = 𝟎 \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0} and 𝐰 frac = 𝟎 \mathbf{w}^{\mathrm{frac}}=\mathbf{0} ,
the architecture reduces exactly to standard KAN.
Initialization sets w frac = 0 w^{\mathrm{frac}}=0 and d raw ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , 0.01 ) d^{\mathrm{raw}}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,0.01) ,
so the network starts as a KAN and develops fractal structure only where the data demands it. (c) α \alpha -fractal decomposition showing the B-spline basis b b (blue),
hybrid outputs b + h b+h at increasing d i d_{i} , and the isolated fractal correction h h (dashed). (d) Recovery property: ‖ 𝐝 ‖ → 0 \|\mathbf{d}\|\to 0 implies f b α → b f_{b}^{\alpha}\to b (KAN recovery). (e) Fractal energy ratio ρ = ‖ w frac ‖ 1 / ‖ w spl ‖ 1 \rho=\|w^{\mathrm{frac}}\|_{1}/\|w^{\mathrm{spl}}\|_{1} across target Hölder exponents: fractal path is active ( ρ ≫ 0 \rho\gg 0 ) on rough targets
and dormant ( ρ ≈ 0 \rho\approx 0 ) on smooth targets.

Figure 7 : Hybrid FI-KAN vs. KAN on four representative targets spanning the regularity spectrum. Top row: target function (gray), KAN fit (blue dashed), and Hybrid FI-KAN fit (red) with final test MSE. Bottom row: training curves (test MSE vs. epoch, log scale) with improvement ratios. On smooth targets (polynomial, exp_sin), Hybrid FI-KAN converges to orders-of-magnitude lower MSE. On fractal targets (Weierstrass, sawtooth), the fractal correction path captures multi-scale structure that B-splines miss entirely.

Figure 8 : Hölder regularity sweep: test MSE as a function of Hölder exponent α \alpha for f α ​ ( x ) = | x | α f_{\alpha}(x)=|x|^{\alpha} . Hybrid FI-KAN achieves the lowest MSE at every regularity level, with improvement factors over KAN increasing from 1.3 × 1.3\times ( α = 0.2 \alpha=0.2 ) to 33 × 33\times ( α = 1.5 \alpha=1.5 ). The three curves are clearly separated across the entire spectrum, with the C 1 C^{1} boundary ( α = 1 \alpha=1 ) marked. Shaded regions show ± 1 \pm 1 std over 5 seeds.

Figure 9 : Scaling laws (Hybrid FI-KAN): test RMSE vs. parameter count across three targets spanning the regularity spectrum. Left (exp_sin, smooth): FI-KAN achieves low error from the smallest grid size, saturating early while KAN continues improving with more parameters. Center (Weierstrass, rough): FI-KAN achieves lower RMSE than KAN at all model sizes, with both showing positive scaling. Right (sawtooth, fractal): MLP completely stagnates. FI-KAN maintains a consistent advantage over KAN at every model size. Scaling exponents fitted over the full grid range G ∈ { 3 , 5 , 8 , 12 , 16 , 20 } G\in\{3,5,8,12,16,20\} are reported in Table 4 . Shaded regions show ± 1 \pm 1 std over 5 seeds.

Figure 10 : Noise robustness on fractal targets (Hybrid FI-KAN). Models trained with noisy data, evaluated on clean test data. Left (Weierstrass): FI-KAN maintains a ∼ 3.5 × \sim\!3.5\times advantage across all SNR levels, with the gap narrowing gracefully under heavy noise. Right (sawtooth): up to 6.1 × 6.1\times advantage at clean SNR, degrading gracefully to 4.7 × 4.7\times at extreme noise ( 5 5 dB). Shaded regions show ± 1 \pm 1 std over 5 seeds.

Figure 11 : Continual learning across 5 sequential tasks (Hybrid FI-KAN). Each column shows one training phase; each row shows a different architecture’s output. Top (Data): the current task’s target function (black) with all tasks shown in gray. MLP: catastrophic forgetting collapses the output to a near-constant function. KAN: retains some structure but progressively distorts earlier tasks. FI-KAN: retains peaked structure across phases, with the fractal and spline paths providing implicit modularity. Quantitative MSE for the task sequence including the corrected Takagi–Landsberg target is reported in Table 6 .

Figure 12 : Regularization sweep (Hybrid FI-KAN) on polynomial (left) and Weierstrass (right) targets. Left (polynomial): test MSE is nearly flat across λ frac \lambda_{\mathrm{frac}} , confirming that the spline path already handles smooth targets effectively; the fractal regularizer has little to correct. Right (Weierstrass): stronger regularization ( λ frac ≥ 10 − 2 \lambda_{\mathrm{frac}}\geq 10^{-2} ) reduces MSE by ∼ 20 % \sim\!20\% , suppressing unnecessary fractal complexity while preserving beneficial multi-scale structure. Shaded regions show ± 1 \pm 1 std. The Hybrid variant is robust to regularization weight choice, in contrast to the Pure variant’s sensitivity ( Table 7 ).

Figure 13 : Impact of fractal recursion depth K K on Hybrid FI-KAN performance (Weierstrass target). Red (left axis): test MSE (log scale) with ± 1 \pm 1 std error bars. Blue dashed (right axis): average training time. K = 2 K=2 achieves optimal MSE at minimal computational cost. Deeper recursion ( K > 4 K>4 ) monotonically degrades performance while linearly increasing training time, reflecting the optimization difficulty of propagating gradients through multiple sequential multiplicative steps.

Figure 14 : 2D function regression (Hybrid FI-KAN). Top row (Ackley): KAN smooths out the multi-modal structure near the origin (MSE 0.0204), while FI-KAN recovers the peaked landscape (MSE 0.0016), a 12.8 × 12.8\times improvement. Bottom row (2D Weierstrass): KAN captures only coarse structure (MSE 0.1577), while FI-KAN resolves finer oscillatory detail (MSE 0.0826), a 1.9 × 1.9\times improvement. Left column: target surface. Center: KAN prediction. Right: Hybrid FI-KAN prediction.

Figure 15 : L-shaped domain benchmark ( − Δ ​ u = 1 -\Delta u=1 , Hölder 2 / 3 2/3 corner singularity). Left: FEM reference solution computed via scikit-fem (P1 elements, 6144 elements);
the singularity at the re-entrant corner is visible as the peaked region near the origin. Center: Test MSE comparison (log scale, ± 1 \pm 1 std over 5 seeds).
Hybrid FI-KAN achieves 3.5 × 3.5\times lower MSE than KAN. Right: Training curves showing Hybrid FI-KAN converging to ∼ 10 − 3 \sim\!10^{-3} MSE
while KAN plateaus near 3 × 10 − 3 3\times 10^{-3} .

Figure 16 : Learned fractal dimension vs. true Hölder exponent (Hybrid FI-KAN). The learned dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} correlates monotonically with target regularity: smooth targets ( α = 2.0 \alpha=2.0 ) yield dim B = 1.0 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1.0 exactly (fractal path inactive), while rough targets ( α = 0.3 \alpha=0.3 , Weierstrass) yield dim B > 1.1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}>1.1 (fractal path active). The dotted line at dim B = 1 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}=1 marks the smooth baseline. This monotonic correlation makes the learned fractal dimension a useful, interpretable proxy for target regularity.

Figure 17 : Fractal parameter evolution during training (Pure FI-KAN). Top row: individual d i d_{i} trajectories for layer 0 across three targets. Bottom row: mean dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} over training. Left (polynomial): the contraction parameters grow steadily, pushing dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} above 1.2; this is the smooth-target overfitting pathology predicted by Corollary 4.8 . Center (Weierstrass): dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} stays near 1.0 throughout, indicating the regularizer successfully constrains fractal complexity. Right (sawtooth): dim B \dim_{\mathrm{B}} spikes to ∼ 1.05 \sim\!1.05 in early training (exploration phase) then settles back, suggesting an explore-then-consolidate dynamic where the network first probes fractal structure and then retains only what reduces the loss.

Figure 18 : KAN paper toy functions (Hybrid FI-KAN). Bar chart comparison of test MSE across five canonical smooth targets. KAN wins on 3 of 5 targets where B-splines are near-optimal. The one Hybrid FI-KAN win (10D sum, ∼ 30 × \sim\!30\times improvement) suggests that the fractal correction provides additional representational diversity that benefits higher-dimensional targets even when the underlying function is smooth.

Figure 19 : Feynman physics equations (Hybrid FI-KAN). Bar chart comparison across seven analytic physics targets. KAN wins 4 of 7, consistent with the expectation that B-splines are well-adapted to analytic targets. The FI-KAN wins (Gaussian, relativity, diffraction) occur on functions with sharp transitions or oscillatory structure that benefit from multi-scale representation.

Figure 20 : Scaling laws (Pure FI-KAN): test RMSE vs. parameter count. Left (exp_sin): Pure FI-KAN exhibits a negative scaling exponent: test error increases with model size. This is the pathological behavior predicted by Corollary 4.8 : additional grid points introduce more oscillatory fractal basis functions, making the cancellation required for smooth approximation increasingly fragile. Center (Weierstrass): mild negative scaling. Right (sawtooth): negative scaling at large grid sizes, extending the smooth-approximation obstruction to fractal targets when too many uncontrolled fractal bases are introduced without the spline backbone. At small-to-moderate grid sizes, positive scaling is observed before the pathology sets in.

Figure 21 : Pure FI-KAN vs. KAN on four representative targets. Top row: function fits. Bottom row: training curves with improvement ratios. The pattern is complementary to Hybrid FI-KAN ( Fig. 7 ): Pure FI-KAN loses badly on smooth targets (ratio 0.0x on polynomial, exp_sin) but wins on the sawtooth (ratio 2.3x). This asymmetry is the strongest controlled evidence for the regularity-matching hypothesis.

Figure 22 : Noise robustness (Pure FI-KAN). The Pure variant also outperforms KAN on fractal targets under noise, though with smaller margins than the Hybrid. Left (Weierstrass): ∼ 1.5 × \sim\!1.5\times advantage. Right (sawtooth): up to 2.2 × 2.2\times advantage at clean SNR.

Figure 23 : Learned fractal dimension vs. true Hölder exponent (Pure FI-KAN). The monotonic correlation is present but weaker than the Hybrid variant ( Fig. 16 ): smooth targets receive dim B ≈ 1.13 \dim_{\mathrm{B}}\approx 1.13 rather than 1.0, because the Pure variant is forced to use some fractal structure even when inappropriate. This systematic overestimation further confirms that fractal-only bases are suboptimal for smooth targets.

Figure 24 : 2D function regression (Pure FI-KAN). Pure FI-KAN loses on Ackley (smooth outside origin) but captures more fine-scale structure on the 2D Weierstrass target than KAN (MSE 0.1251 vs. 0.1871), consistent with the regularity-matching thesis.
